steps.title: Identify the mechanism of fungal spread
steps.content:
The text states the fungus “can spread from tree to nearby tree through the air.” Proximity is the key: the fungus can only reach trees close enough for airborne transmission. The surviving Nacionals were in “a hard-to-reach valley,” signaling geographic isolation. Any explanation for their survival must connect isolation to the proximity-dependent spread.
  • Choice A — 'early twentieth-century scientists did not know why so many Nacionals were becoming infected.': Scientists’ ignorance of the disease does not explain how physically isolated trees avoided infection. ✗
  • Choice B — 'the ability of the fungus to travel through the air was only recently discovered.': When the airborne ability was discovered is irrelevant to whether the fungus physically reached trees in a remote valley in the past. ✗
  • Choice C — 'they were too far from the other Nacional trees infected by the fungus to become infected themselves.': Distance prevented the airborne fungus from bridging the gap to these trees — a direct consequence of the “nearby tree” spread mechanism. ✓
  • Choice D — 'the chocolate made from their pods was highly valued.': The value of chocolate is an economic fact unrelated to how the fungus spreads or why these trees were spared. ✗

steps.title: Confirm the correct choice
steps.content: Because the fungus spreads only to nearby trees, being geographically isolated in a remote valley is exactly what would have prevented infection. That matches choice C — C is correct.
